title: Positive vs Negative Instructions
concept: Telling a model what TO do outperforms telling it what NOT to do; reframing negative constraints positively.

exercises.prompt: Quick recap: why does a vague prompt like 'write something about dogs' usually produce a vague answer?

options.text: Because the model doesn't know enough about dogs
Because without specific constraints, format, or examples, the model has to guess what you want
Because vague prompts use too many tokens

exercises.explain: Right! Vague prompts leave too much open to interpretation, so the model fills the gaps with generic guesses. Adding specifics — like format, length, or examples — narrows down what a 'good' answer looks like.

cards.title: Don't Think About a Pink Elephant!
cards.body: Quick — now you're picturing a pink elephant, aren't you? Telling someone (or something) what NOT to do often makes them focus on exactly that thing. Language models have the same quirk, and it shows up in your prompts.

cards.title: Positive vs Negative Instructions
cards.body: A positive instruction tells the model what TO do (e.g., 'Answer in one sentence'). A negative instruction tells it what NOT to do (e.g., 'Don't be too long'). Positive instructions almost always work better because they give the model a clear target instead of just a forbidden zone.

cards.title: Like Coaching a Runner
cards.body: A coach who yells "Don't trip on the hurdles!" makes the runner think about tripping. A coach who says "Lift your knees high!" gives the runner something to actually DO. Models are the same: they run better toward a clear target than away from a vague danger.

cards.title: Same Goal, Two Prompts
cards.body: Negative: 'Don't write a boring product description.' Result: still vague — boring compared to what? Positive: 'Write an exciting product description using vivid sensory words and one surprising fact.' Result: the model knows exactly what to aim for and delivers it.

cards.title: Myth: 'Don't' Instructions Are Just as Clear
cards.body: Myth: Saying 'Don't use jargon' is just as effective as saying 'Use simple everyday words.' Truth: negative rules only rule things out — they don't tell the model what to replace them with, so it may still miss the mark. Positive rules define the actual target behavior, which models can follow much more reliably.

cards.title: Negative Fence vs Positive Path
cards.body: A negative instruction is like a fence marking where NOT to go — it leaves a huge open field of 'anywhere else' to wander. A positive instruction is like a marked path showing exactly where to walk. Paths get you to the destination faster than fences alone.

left.text: "Don't be vague or too formal." — model still doesn't know what tone to use.

right.text: "Write in a warm, conversational tone with specific examples." — model has a clear path to follow.

exercises.prompt: Which of these is the BEST reframe of the negative instruction 'Don't write more than a few sentences'?

options.text: Write no more than 3 sentences total.
Avoid excessive length in your response.

exercises.explain: 'Write no more than 3 sentences total' turns a vague negative ('don't be too long') into a precise positive target with a concrete number, so the model knows exactly what success looks like.

exercises.prompt: True or False: 'Don't include any negative words in your review' is generally a more effective instruction than 'Write a review using only positive and encouraging language.'
exercises.explain: False — the positive version wins. Telling the model to avoid 'negative words' is vague and forces it to check against an undefined blacklist, while 'use positive and encouraging language' gives it a clear, actionable style to produce.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank with the correct word.
exercises.sentence: Instead of writing 'Don't use technical terms,' a stronger prompt says 'Use simple, everyday words a beginner can understand,' because positive instructions give the model a clear target.
exercises.explain: 'Target' is correct because positive instructions describe the goal the model should aim for, rather than just a boundary to avoid — giving it something concrete to produce.

exercises.prompt: Two students rewrote the same instruction to be more positive. One rewrite was generated by an AI and sounds smooth but generic; the other was written by a human student. Spot the AI-generated one.

options.text: Write a 2-sentence summary that includes the main character's name and the story's final outcome.
Craft a concise and engaging summary that effectively captures the essence of the narrative in a positive manner.
Summarize the story in exactly two sentences, naming the hero and how the story ends.

exercises.explain: Option B is the AI-flavored one — it's full of smooth but empty phrases like 'effectively captures the essence' and 'in a positive manner' without giving any concrete, checkable target. The other two options give specific, testable requirements (sentence count, name, outcome), which is what a thoughtful human editor would produce.

exercises.prompt: You're writing a system prompt for a kids' homework helper bot. Which instruction set best follows the 'tell it what TO do' principle?

options.text: Don't be rude. Don't give wrong answers. Don't use big words. Don't ignore the question.
Never talk about anything scary or inappropriate for kids.
Speak kindly and encouragingly. Give accurate, step-by-step answers. Use simple words a 10-year-old understands. Always address the student's question directly.
Avoid being boring or too short in your replies.

exercises.explain: Option C wins because every line tells the model exactly what behavior to produce: kind tone, accurate steps, simple words, direct answers. The other options are lists of things to avoid, which leave the model guessing what a 'good' response actually looks like — it can dodge the negative without hitting any real target. Positive, concrete instructions give the model a destination, not just a list of potholes to swerve around.
